cataloging_subject.label: Altenglische Legenden: Neue Folge
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is Carl Horstmann’s 1881 edited selection of sixty-five medieval verse items, not one medieval work, one manuscript, or the complete contents of every cited witness. The German editorial prose, source notes, alternate witness versions, and a poem embedded in the back notes remain explicit parts of this edition-level boundary.

catalog_summary.title: Altenglische Legenden: Neue Folge
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous medieval authors; John Lydgate for three scoped items
catalog_summary.date: Medieval texts of varied dates; edited selection first published in 1881
catalog_summary.region: England and Scotland; composition and dialect regions vary by item

notes: The preferred title follows Horstmann’s title page. In this nineteenth-century usage, Altenglisch encompasses texts now cataloged as Middle English and Older Scots; it does not mean that the contents are Old English.
A literal supplied English translation of the printed German title, not a claim that the medieval texts are in Old English.

scope.description: All cataloged verse items except the three securely assigned here to John Lydgate.

notes: Horstmann’s inherited label 'Barbour’s Legendensammlung' for Saint Machor is not converted into secure authorship by John Barbour.
Compiler refers to the constitution of this 1881 multi-source edited selection, not authorship of the medieval works.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth to first half of the fifteenth century (Harley MS 4196 and Cotton MS Tiberius E VII)

assertion.notes: A bounded attestation range for the represented Northern Legendary witnesses, not a shared composition date for all thirty-two cataloged items.

date_statements.display: Fifteenth century in Cambridge University Library MS Gg.2.6

assertion.notes: Dates the represented witness broadly; it does not supply an exact Saint Machor composition date.

date_statements.display: Probably slightly after 1386 and no later than 1420

assertion.notes: Only the explicit upper bound is normalized; ‘slightly after 1386’ is not converted into an invented exact lower year.

date_statements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century in the sole witness, Harley MS 2250

places.label: Aberdeen area, Scotland
notes: Used only for Horstmann’s historical dialect characterization of Saint Machor, not as a proved place of composition.

places.label: Cheshire, England
notes: Used only for the work-specific linguistic profile of Saint Erkenwald in Harley MS 2250, not as a proved authorial place of composition.

scope.description: Horstmann’s Northern Legendary grouping; a textual and dialect-region label, not a single place of composition for all thirty-two items.

assertion.notes: This is scoped to the Older Scots Saint Machor stream and does not make Scotland the origin of the whole selection.
A historical linguistic attribution retained with qualification; it is not converted into an exact composition place.

scope.description: Language of Saint Erkenwald in its sole surviving witness.

assertion.notes: The work-specific eLALME profile does not establish where the poem was composed.

scope.description: The medieval verse items other than the explicitly Older Scots Saint Machor.
Horstmann’s titles, introductions, source notes, and apparatus.
Rubrics, titles, source quotations, and short embedded passages; no separately cataloged Latin work item is present.

form_statements.description: The cataloging subject is intentionally Horstmann’s edited selection as a whole, which retains sixty-five verse items together with substantial German editorial prose, apparatus, and source commentary. Every medieval item part is separately typed as verse.

scope.description: All sixty-five cataloged medieval text items.
Dominant but not exclusive: saints’ lives and feast legends are interspersed with devotional, visionary, and exemplary verse.

subject_statements.label: Parallel manuscript versions and textual transmission

summaries.value: Carl Horstmann’s 1881 Altenglische Legenden: Neue Folge is cataloged here as a multi-witness scholarly edited selection containing sixty-five medieval verse text items alongside retained German editorial prose, source notes, and textual apparatus. Its intellectual boundary is the edition’s selected streams and recensions, not one medieval work or one manuscript.

Horstmann’s volume brings together a Northern Legendary sequence, two first-appendix lives, a broad group of single legends, a final appendix, and one independent Thornton poem embedded in the notes. The sixty-five-item count treats separately presented intellectual texts as items, counts the two hell stories and two Marian fragments separately, but does not double-count alternate versions of Alexius, Katherine, or Edmund and Fremund. A complete DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk matches sixty-four of the sixty-five items to sixty-seven unique records through seventy part-to-record mappings; repertory overlaps are recorded without replacing the edition-defined item boundary.

The U-M page-image object has 708 canvas positions but exactly 674 unique image payloads, reconciling the XML header’s 674 source TIFF images. It includes omitted front matter, the unencoded Early Modern Saint Eustace on printed pp. 472–487, and unencoded notes, corrigenda, and contents; these image-layer materials are not silently promoted into the sixty-five encoded medieval text items. Likewise, DIMEV record-2003 identifies the uncounted eight-line prologue and records 755 and 960 identify front-matter quotations, none of which changes the count.

extent.description: Sixty-five cataloged medieval verse items. The raw XML contains 11 DIV1, 66 DIV2, 37 DIV3, 1,013 LG, and 34,045 L elements. The L total includes an eight-line uncounted prologue framing the Northern Legendary; the item-level metrics sum to 34,037 lines. The three zero-line numbered headings for Purification, Annunciation, and Nativity of John are German editorial notices, not medieval text items.

content_parts.label: I. The Northern Legendary collection
content_parts.summary: Thirty-one numbered Middle English verse legends edited chiefly from British Library Harley MS 4196, with Cotton MS Tiberius E VII variants, plus a separately sourced Ashmole 42 sermon; the eight-line prologue is retained as framing matter rather than counted as a work item.
notes: The parent block includes verse texts together with German editorial headings, source notes, or apparatus.

content_parts.label: Northern Legendary prologue
content_parts.summary: An eight-line framing prologue to the Northern Legendary sequence; retained as verse context but not counted among the sixty-five medieval text items.

locators.scheme: DIMEV v1.0 record and witness rows

notes: Its zero item_count preserves the sixty-five-item boundary while reconciling all 34,045 XML L elements.
DIMEV v1.0 record-2003 independently identifies this eight-line Northern Legendary prologue; it remains framing matter with item_count 0 and does not inflate the sixty-five counted items.

related_works.title: Le Château d’Amour
notes: This relation applies only to part:creation. Horstmann describes De Principio Creationis Mundi as a Middle English rendering associated with Robert Grosseteste through a French intermediary; it does not make Grosseteste the author of the whole edited selection.

evidence.locator: HEADER; TITLEPAGE; complete FRONT, BODY, and BACK
evidence.summary: Identifies U-M item AFW1383.0001.001 and preserves Horstmann’s section hierarchy, source headings, printed-page breaks, medieval texts, variants, and back-matter poem.

evidence.locator: Element counts and complete division tree
evidence.summary: Counts 11 DIV1, 66 DIV2, 37 DIV3, 1,013 LG, 34,045 L, 168 MILESTONE, 108 P, and 708 PB occurrences. The PB elements contain 674 unique REF values: 640 occur once and 34 occur twice, reconciling the header’s 674 source TIFFs without inflating the carrier. The audit also distinguishes the eight-line prologue, three zero-line editorial headings, sixty-five verse items, paired versions, split stories, and notes poem.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for University of Michigan AFW1383.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Supports the public item identity, title, editor, publication statement, call number, OCR/encoding history, and current access statement.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for the U-M 708-position page-image manifest.
evidence.summary: Supports the complete public sequence, exact canvas ranges, and checksum audit showing 674 unique images among 708 positions.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from the complete 1881 Horstmann edition scan.
evidence.summary: Supports the printed title, editor, date, item boundaries, source statements, page ranges, German apparatus, and intermediary Hickes chain.

evidence.citation: British Library catalog records for Harley MS 4196 and Cotton MS Tiberius E VII.
evidence.summary: Supports the current witness identities and the late-fourteenth/early-fifteenth-century Harley range and first-half-fifteenth-century Tiberius range used only for the Northern Legendary’s bounded attestation statement.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.2.6, historical university catalog description.
evidence.summary: Supports the current shelfmark, fifteenth-century witness context, Scottish Legendary contents, and the bounded Saint Machor attestation statement.

evidence.citation: Stefan Schustereder, ‘Coming to Terms with a Pagan Past: The Story of St Erkenwald,’ Studia Anglica Posnaniensia 48.2–3 (2013), 71–92.
evidence.summary: Supports the probably-post-1386/no-later-than-1420 composition discussion, sole witness, witness date, and the need to distinguish composition chronology from manuscript production.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 706 and manuscript entry BLHar2250.
evidence.summary: Supports Saint Erkenwald’s sole witness at Harley MS 2250, ff. 72v–75v, and work-specific eLALME Linguistic Profile 419 localization to Cheshire.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2250, record 040-002048081.
evidence.summary: Supports Saint Erkenwald at ff. 72v–75v and the second-half-fifteenth-century date of its sole witness.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse (DIMEV), version 1.0 dataset, Zenodo release 14585961; complete AFW1383 crosswalk against Records.xml.
evidence.locator: digital-index-of-middle-english-verse-dimev-74d7c48/data/Records.xml; record-2003 for the uncounted prologue; exact record and witness-row identifiers in each content-part locator
evidence.summary: Checks all sixty-five counted verse items against DIMEV v1.0: sixty-four items match, one (Saint Peter in Chains) has no defensible exact match, sixty-seven unique DIMEV records support seventy part-to-record mappings, and record-2003 identifies the uncounted eight-line Northern Legendary prologue. Reused records 2652, 383, and 317 and multi-record items are retained as overlaps rather than allowed to alter the edition-defined sixty-five-item boundary. Records 755 and 960 describe front-matter quotations, not counted items. The audit also records source-reference anomalies for records 364, 5690, 5800, and 3348.

open_questions.question: Can the Trinity College Cambridge witness behind Hickes’s Meidan Maregrete be identified in a current catalog, or is it lost?
next_steps: Compare Hickes’s wording and historical Trinity catalogs with current Cambridge shelfmarks; seek repository confirmation before assigning a modern identifier.
notes: The lineage retains the chain Horstmann → Hickes 1705 → reported Trinity witness instead of collapsing Horstmann directly onto an unidentified manuscript.

open_questions.question: Can complete public or commissioned whole-object facsimiles be obtained for the uncached British Library, Cambridge, Durham, and Lincoln witnesses?
next_steps: Use the recorded catalog and reproduction routes; request complete objects rather than work-only crops, then map the target loci.

open_questions.question: What are the defensible item-level composition dates, cultural origins, and dialect regions for the text streams not covered by the scoped Northern Legendary, Saint Machor, and Saint Erkenwald statements?
next_steps: The full DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk is complete; now consult item-specific editions, linguistic profiles, and scholarship for composition, attestation, dialect, and origin claims that DIMEV does not itself settle.
Record composition, first-attestation, dialect, and witness-production claims as separate statements; do not substitute witness dates or holding locations for composition evidence.
Add only part-scoped assertions whose exact evidence and uncertainty can be cited.
notes: The broad catalog summary intentionally says that item dates and regions vary; it is not a normalized whole-selection medieval date or place claim.

open_questions.question: Can the Saint Peter in Chains item be assigned a defensible DIMEV/NIMEV identity beyond DIMEV v1.0?
next_steps: Check later DIMEV releases and the print NIMEV, then pursue incipit and source-specific scholarship for the Harley 4196 and Cotton Tiberius E VII text.
Do not assign record-2652: its incipit and source span begin with the following Sixtus/Lawrence sequence.
notes: This is one unmatched item in a complete sixty-five-item DIMEV v1.0 check, not evidence that the text is absent from all repertories.

The repository XML language header says only ger; this record corrects that encoding-level simplification by cataloging substantive Middle English, Older Scots, editorial German, and embedded Latin separately.

The three zero-line numbered headings at Purification, Annunciation, and the Nativity of John the Baptist are excluded from the item count because they contain German editorial source notes but no encoded medieval text.
John Barbour appears in inherited U-M bibliographic metadata and Horstmann’s historical collection label; neither is treated as secure whole-selection or Saint Machor authorship.
The XML milestone 'Nero CXII fol. 181' is preserved as legacy evidence, but the current British Library component record maps the Wulfhad and Ruffin text to Cotton Nero C XII, ff. 183r–188v.

The sixty-five text items are cataloged item/version streams within this edited selection. The count is not an assertion that every stream is a wholly distinct abstract medieval work: alternate Alexius, Katherine, and Edmund/Fremund recensions are deliberately not double-counted, while independently presented hell stories and Marian fragments are counted separately.

The complete DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk checks all sixty-five counted verse items: sixty-four match sixty-seven unique records through seventy part-to-record mappings, while Saint Peter in Chains remains explicitly unmatched. Record-2003 maps the uncounted eight-line prologue; records 755 and 960 are front-matter quotations and do not alter the sixty-five-item boundary.
